title_zh: 一个完美的镀金时代甜品
rubric_zh: 美国如何庆祝它的100岁生日

paragraphs.en: William Dean Howells, the editor of The Atlantic, wandered through Philadelphia’s Centennial Exhibition of 1876 trying to make sense of a spectacle that defied description. Two wheels, one small, one large, seemed to tell the story of the great transformation on display. The small one was made of wood—an old spinning wheel set up in a rude log cabin meant to conjure colonial Plymouth. As Howells related, a reenactor playing the Mayflower pilgrim Priscilla Alden paused in her work to give an old Quaker woman a turn. At first, the woman’s “long-unwonted fingers” seemed rusty. She struggled to splice the thread, then got it tangled while Howells and others watched in breathless silence. Finally, though, her dexterity revived, and the wheel came to life “with a soft triumphant burr, while the crowd heaved a sigh of relief.” It was, Howells reflected, “altogether the prettiest thing I saw at the Centennial.”
paragraphs.zh: 《大西洋》杂志的编辑威廉·迪安·霍尔斯在费城1876年的百年博览会上穿梭，努力理解这场难以用语言形容的奇观。两只纺轮，一大一小，仿佛在讲述展出的巨大变迁史。小的那只是木头做的——一台旧纺纱轮，放在一间粗陋的木屋里，想营造殖民时代普利茅斯的氛围。霍尔斯叙述道，一位扮演“五月花号”朝圣者普莉希拉·奥尔登的重演者，停下手中的动作，让一位年长的贵格会女信徒上前试纺。起初，这位老妇人的“久未运动的手指”似乎生了锈，纱线难以接续，随后又在霍尔斯和其他围观者屏住呼吸的注视下打了个结。最终，她的灵巧又恢复了，纺轮发出“低柔而胜利的轻鸣”，周围人群也“松了一口气”。霍尔斯感慨道，这“完全是我在百年博览会上见到的最可爱的景象”。

paragraphs.en: But Howells and millions of other Americans went to Philadelphia as much to look forward as to look back. Far more thrilling than the wooden spinning wheel was the huge cast-iron wheel—30 feet in diameter and 122,000 pounds—turning almost noiselessly at the center of the complex works of the Corliss Engine in Machinery Hall, driven by steam pumped in from a separate building. Howells sat, stunned, before the engine’s “infinitely varied machinery” working “with unerring intelligence.” No Priscilla Alden or old Quaker was necessary here—just a single, mostly idle attendant whose only job was to occasionally put down his newspaper and administer a few drops of oil.
paragraphs.zh: 但霍尔斯和数以百万计其他美国人去费城，既是为了回望过去，也同样是为了展望未来。远比那只木纺轮更令人心跳的是那台巨大的铸铁轮——直径30英尺、重122,000磅——它在机械大厅内的柯利斯发动机复杂装置中心几乎无声地转动，由另一栋楼内输送的蒸汽驱动。霍尔斯坐在那里，震撼地注视着这台“变化无穷的机器”以“无可置疑的聪明”协同运转。在这里，不需要普莉希拉·奥尔登，也不需要那位老贵格会妇女；只需一个大多数时候都闲着的引导员，偶尔把报纸放下，滴几滴机油即可。

paragraphs.en: From the July 1876 issue: William Dean Howells on a sennight of the Centennial
paragraphs.zh: 《1876年7月刊》：威廉·迪安·霍尔斯在百年展会的一周

paragraphs.en: In Centennial: The Great Fair of 1876 and the Invention of America’s Future, the historian Fergus M. Bordewich takes a tour of the exhibition—and, venturing beyond it, takes the measure of America on its 100th birthday. At not quite 200 pages of text, the book is brisk and tightly constructed, filled with vivid characters and finely wrought, often-wrenching scenes. Along the way, Bordewich finds a country caught between the marvel of its material progress—what he calls a “phantasmagorical theater of national glory”—and the fragility of its ideals and institutions. While the fair celebrated a confident industrial future, the nation outside its gates seethed with violence, corruption, and social inequality.
paragraphs.zh: 在《百年展会：1876年伟大博览会与美国未来的诞生》中，历史学家弗格斯·M·博代维奇带我们参观了该展会，并且在展会之外继续前行，给美国在100岁生日时做了整体评估。全书不足200页，节奏清快、结构严谨，人物鲜明，场景写得细腻而常常刺痛人心。沿途，博代维奇发现美国此刻夹在两极之间：一边是其物质进步带来的奇迹——他称之为“国家荣耀的幻影剧场”——另一边是其理想与制度的脆弱。展会在高歌工业化前景的同时，这个国家在展馆外部却又在血与火、腐败与社会不平等中翻腾。

paragraphs.en: Writing in our own anniversary moment, when historical narratives are themselves the stuff of cultural and political conflict, Bordewich largely lets the discord speak for itself. But taken together, the dizzying disorientations of 1876 can’t help fueling grim conclusions. Bordewich, who has written forceful histories of the Underground Railroad, the radicalism of the Republican Congress during the Civil War, and Reconstruction-era Ku Klux Klan violence, presents a country turning its back on two revolutions—those of 1776 and 1865. In their place, Americans embraced a third: an industrial revolution that rendered the spinning wheel a quaint relic of the past and the iron wheel an object of faith for the future.
paragraphs.zh: 在我们自己的周年纪念时刻，历史叙事本身已经成为文化与政治冲突的燃点，波德威奇（Bordewich）大多让这些冲突自行发声。然而，将1876年的一系列令人眩晕的错位和混乱拼合在一起时，人们难免得出一种阴郁的结论。波德威奇曾写过关于地下铁路、内战期间共和党国会的激进性，以及重建时期三K党暴力活动的有力著作，他在书中呈现的是一个转身背离两场革命的国家：1776年的革命和1865年的革命。取而代之的是，美国人拥抱了第三次革命——工业革命，它使纺轮成了过去的温雅遗物，而将铁轮奉为通往未来的信仰象征。

paragraphs.en: The centennial commenced on the morning of May 10, 1876, with a seemingly providential break in the rain. The program—which included lengthy speeches on the marvels of American growth, and an orchestra playing an array of national anthems from around the world in addition to Richard Wagner’s made-to-order “American Centennial March”—had swelled to fit the exhibition’s hybrid nature. What had originally been intended as a national birthday celebration had been joined to a World’s Fair displaying the material wonders of the Industrial Age. The ensuing event, which ran for the next six months, was designed to show how happily material progress and national glory could be made to blend. Financed through a mix of public and private money and enabled in no small part by the backroom maneuvering of the Pennsylvania Railroad boss Thomas Scott—whose lines stood to benefit from the traffic—it was a perfect Gilded Age confection.
paragraphs.zh: 百年庆典于1876年5月10日早晨拉开序幕，一场看似天降的停雨让开幕得以开始。展会节目单早已扩展，既有关于美国发展奇迹的冗长演讲，也有管弦乐团演奏世界各国国歌，除此之外还加入了理查德·瓦格纳（Richard Wagner）按需创作的《美国百年纪念进行曲》。这场活动的规模与内容不断膨胀，反映出其混合性格。原本仅被设想为国家生日庆典的活动，最终与一场展示工业时代物质奇观的世界博览会结合在一起。随后的六个月里举行的展会，意在展示物质进步与国家荣光如何可以和谐融合。它由公共与私人资金共同筹措，而在很大程度上得益于宾夕法尼亚铁路大亨托马斯·斯科特（Thomas Scott）在幕后运作——其铁路线路将因客流而受益；这正是一个典型的“镀金时代甜点”。

paragraphs.en: The massive glass-and-iron main building was the largest man-made structure on the planet at the time, encompassing more than 21 acres of floor space. It was just one of some 200 buildings across the site, many of them sparkling architectural showpieces dedicated to, among other things, pomology, photography, brewing, dairy processing, and glassmaking. Nations and states commissioned their own buildings—a Tudor mansion for England, a residence and “bazaar” for Japan, a “cottage” for Connecticut, a Spanish-moss-fringed log cabin for Mississippi. Fairgoers could see some of the first mechanical typewriters in action, sample Charles E. Hires’s root beer, hear the ear-splitting “annunciator” of Western Electric’s new heat-sensitive fire alarm.
paragraphs.zh: 那座巨大的玻璃与铁结构主楼当时是世界上最大的人工建筑，占地面积超过21英亩。它只是在整个展区约200座建筑中的一座，其余许多都是闪亮的建筑展示品，主题包括果树栽培（pomology）、摄影、啤酒酿造、奶制品加工和玻璃制造等。各国和各州都建造了自己的展馆：英格兰有一座都铎式庄园，日本有一处住宅与“市集”，康涅狄格州有一座“小屋”，密西西比州有一座带有西班牙地衣装饰边缘的木屋。参观者可以亲眼见到最早期的机械打字机在现场运转，品尝查尔斯·E·海尔斯（Charles E. Hires）的根汁汽水，还能听到西部电器公司新型感温火警系统那刺耳的“警报器”。

paragraphs.en: When the exhibition closed in November, at least 9,799,392 people—about 20 percent of the U.S. population—had visited, including a 79-year-old man who walked from New Albany, Indiana, with the aid of a stick cut and carved in the 1810s. Most of those who went were delightfully overwhelmed. In a letter home, a young woman from Providence, Rhode Island, struggled to find words for what she saw: “Dear Mother, Oh! Oh!! Oh!!! Oh!!!! Oh!!!!! O-o-o-o o-o-o-h!!!!!! Your affectionate daughter, Mary.” Even Alexander Graham Bell, whose newly invented telephone was one of the fair’s sensations, said that it was all “so prodigious and wonderful that it absolutely staggers one.”
paragraphs.zh: 展览于11月闭幕时，至少有9,799,392人到访，占当时美国人口的约20%，其中包括一位79岁的老人，他拄着一根在1810年代锯制并雕刻过的拐杖，从印第安纳州新阿尔巴尼步行而来。大多数来宾都满心欢喜、感到震撼。来自罗得岛州普罗维登斯的一位年轻女子在写给母亲的信里，几乎找不到合适的词形容所见所闻：“亲爱的妈妈，哦！哦！！哦！！！哦！！！！哦！！！！！欧——欧——欧——哦——哦——哦——！” 甚至连亚历山大·格拉汉姆·贝尔（Alexander Graham Bell）也感叹不已：作为展会上轰动一时的新发明，电话的确“是如此巨大而奇妙，以至于令人难以置信地感到震撼”。

paragraphs.en: Behind the unspeakable wonders of Machinery Hall stood a world of labor—dirty, dangerous, and low-paid—that constituted a grave threat to the values of the republic. Such toil clashed with Jeffersonian ideals that equated independent proprietorship of farms and shops with the moral virtue necessary for honest citizenship. Just before the Civil War, Abraham Lincoln had said that men performing wage labor for more than a short stint suffered from “either a dependent nature which prefers it, or improvidence, folly, or singular misfortune.”
paragraphs.zh: 在机械大厅那些无法言表的奇迹背后，存在着一个肮脏、危险且低薪的劳动世界，它构成了对共和国价值观的严峻威胁。这种苦作业与杰斐逊主义的观念发生了冲突：该观念把独立拥有农场和作坊的私人经营者视为正直公民所必需的道德品格。就在南北战争爆发前，亚伯拉罕·林肯曾说，长期从事计酬劳动的人，要么是“有一种依赖天性并偏爱这种生活”，要么是轻率、愚蠢，或是极其不幸。

paragraphs.en: By 1876, industrial wage labor was the permanent misfortune of a growing class of people. Their absence from the spectacle in Machinery Hall was telling. The ingenuity of those who’d designed and displayed the machines fit easily on the trajectory of national greatness; the drudgery of those who did the grunt work off-site did not. The old ethos that Lincoln had believed in still had enough purchase that workers themselves bore the blame for their own miseries. “Had an unfortunate accident this morning,” a Pittsburgh plant manager working for Andrew Carnegie reported (one of Bordewich’s many well-chosen quotes).
paragraphs.zh: 到1876年，工业工资劳动已成为一个不断扩大的阶层的长期不幸。正是这种情况在机械大厅的盛会中不见其身，颇具意味。那些设计并展示机器的人所体现的创造力，很容易融入国家崛起的叙事轨道；而在展区外进行体力苦役的人们所经历的枯燥劳作则不能。林肯所坚信的旧有伦理仍有一定影响力，以至于劳动者本身被归因于自身痛苦的原因。“今天早上发生了一起不幸事故，”安德鲁·卡内基的一位匹兹堡工厂经理报告道（这是博德维奇众多用得极其恰当的引文之一）。

paragraphs.en: Events unfolding that summer in Pennsylvania coal country, about 100 miles away from the exhibition, showed how little hope there was to at least improve working conditions through unions and other forms of labor organizing. As Bordewich narrates in an astonishing set piece, the Philadelphia and Reading Coal and Iron Company had seen to that. When it sought retribution against Irish miners who had gone on strike the previous year, it didn’t merely have the backing of public authority in Schuylkill County; it was the public authority. With the help of a private police force, the company’s president took on the powers of a district attorney and personally prosecuted a set of cases against the miners, most of whom were labor activists. In a series of show trials, the men were charged with murder and assorted acts of terror, and 20 were sent to the gallows.
paragraphs.zh: 当时在距离展会约100英里的宾夕法尼亚煤矿区正在发生的事情表明，即便通过工会和其他劳动组织形式，至少也几乎没有希望改善工人处境。博德维奇在一段令人惊叹的章节叙事中写道，费城与雷丁煤铁公司已经做到了这一点。当公司对上一年罢工的爱尔兰矿工进行报复时，它不仅得到了舒伊尔基尔县公共权力的支持；它本身就是公共权力。凭借一支私人警队，公司总裁掌握起地方检察官的职权，亲自对矿工提起诉讼，其中多数是劳动活动分子。在一系列‘示范性审讯’中，这些人被控谋杀和各种恐怖罪行，20人被送上绞刑架。

paragraphs.en: If industrial workers were largely absent from the centennial, Bordewich shows how Native Americans turned into a different kind of disappearing act. Assumed for decades to be “vanishing” before the tide of Anglo-American civilization, they had become objects of ethnological interest, their “primitive” cultures underscoring the marvels of the present age. Taking the lead on the event’s many Native American exhibits, the Smithsonian’s director, Joseph Henry, first planned to stage a living display of several hundred Native Americans—a Museum of Natural History diorama brought to life. But ongoing hostilities in the West got in the way. Instead, Henry sent expeditions to gather as many Native American artifacts as possible, in order to “present savage life and conditions in all grades and places.” For fairgoers, the juxtaposition with mechanical developments was powerful.
paragraphs.zh: 如果工业工人在百年庆典中基本缺席，博德维奇也展示出另一种“消失”戏法发生在美洲原住民身上。几十年来，人们认为他们会在盎格鲁—美式文明的浪潮中“消亡”，于是他们逐渐成为人类学兴趣的对象，“原始”文化反而被拿来衬托当下时代的奇迹。史密森学会馆长约瑟夫·亨利在展会上领导众多美洲原住民展项，起初计划安排数百名原住民进行“活体展览”——让自然历史博物馆的场景重现为生。可持续不断的西部冲突打断了这项计划。最后，亨利改派考察队尽可能搜集美洲原住民文物，以“在各个层次和地域展示‘野蛮’的生活与生计”。对于参观者而言，与机械进步并置后的对照效果极其强烈。

paragraphs.en: Beyond the exhibition, of course, the “vanishing” of Native Americans was not an abstraction but a policy. Writing on “the Indian question” in 1873, Commissioner of Indian Affairs Francis A. Walker had asked, “What shall be done with the Indian as an obstacle to the national progress?” The answer, he hoped, would involve peaceful means, albeit with extant Western Indians coerced onto reservations, where they would be subject to a “rigid reformatory control” while learning to adopt Anglo-American culture and practices. More often, the putative “Peace Policy” devolved into war, as was the case with the American campaign against the Lakotas happening during the centennial.
paragraphs.zh: 当然，除展览本身之外，“印第安人消失”并不是抽象概念，而是一项政策。1873年，印第安事务专员弗朗西斯·A·沃克在讨论“印第安人问题”时曾问道：“如何处理那些阻碍国家进步的印第安人？”他希望答案会采取和平手段，即便手段是把当时仍在西部的印第安人强行迁入保留地，在那里在“严格的改造性管控”之下学习并接受盎格鲁—美国人的文化与习俗。更常见的是，所谓“和平政策”却演变成战争，就像百年庆典期间对拉科塔人的美国军事行动一样。

paragraphs.en: That campaign—provoked by America’s violation of an existing treaty after the discovery of gold in the Black Hills—produced a great shock at the fair. Just three days after the triumphant Fourth of July celebrations in Philadelphia, news arrived that the Lakotas had routed American forces under the command of General George Armstrong Custer near the Little Bighorn River, in the Montana territory. Custer, who was killed along with more than 260 of his men, had confidently predicted that “civilization in its advancing tread” would “roll mercilessly over” the Plains Indians. Only weeks before, he’d been at the centennial—to take in the wonders of civilization in its advancing tread.
paragraphs.zh: 这场以美国在黑山发现黄金后、因违反现有条约而引发的行动，在博览会上引起了巨大震动。费城那场欢庆的独立日活动胜利落幕后仅三天，消息传来：拉科塔人已在蒙大拿领地的小大角河附近击溃了由乔治·阿姆斯特朗·卡斯特将军指挥的美军。卡斯特与他麾下260多名士兵一同阵亡前，曾自信地预言，“日益前进的文明”会“无情地席卷”平原上的印第安人。就在几周前，他还在百年展会上——在那里他曾去“观赏日益前进的文明奇观”。

paragraphs.en: For Black Americans, the centennial posed a pressing question: What place would they have in the national future it claimed to celebrate? Some, such as Representative Josiah Walls of Florida, anticipated that the fair would blot out “all questions of minor differences and all hurtful recollections of past disagreements.” Walls was mostly right, just not in the way he hoped. Bordewich describes how, at a moment when political support for Reconstruction was ebbing, the exhibition staged a reconciliation not between races, but between regions. White northerners and white southerners restored their old bonds of friendship through symbolic gestures—Union and Confederate generals seated together; a relative of Robert E. Lee reading the Declaration of Independence on the Fourth of July.
paragraphs.zh: 对美国黑人来说，百年庆典提出了一个迫切问题：在它所宣称庆祝的国家未来中，他们将处于什么位置？一些人，如佛罗里达州众议员乔赛亚·沃尔斯，预期这场展会会抹去“所有次要分歧的问题”和“过去争执中一切令人受伤的回忆”。沃尔斯大体上是对的，只是不是他所希望的那种方式。博德威奇指出，正当重建时代的政治支持正在减弱之际，展览展现的“和解”并非种族之间的和解，而是区域之间的和解。白人北方人和白人南方人通过象征性举动恢复了旧日友谊；联盟军与邦联将领同台而坐；南北战争中罗伯特·E·李的一位亲属在7月4日宣读《独立宣言》。

paragraphs.en: Read: Jake Lundberg on an unsettling anti-slavery memorial
paragraphs.zh: 阅读：Jake Lundberg 关于一座令人不安的反奴隶制纪念碑

paragraphs.en: Still, claiming their place in both the exhibition and the nation at large remained a goal of Black Americans. Members of the African Methodist Episcopal Church commissioned a monument to Richard Allen, a prominent Black Philadelphian of the early republic and the founder of the denomination. “We intend to leave Philadelphia in 1876 as did the heroes in 1776, with a fixed resolve to achieve noble results,” the group announced. “And in 1976, we expect our progeny to gather around the Monument in question, shed tears of gratitude for the example we have left them, and call us blessed.” Delayed first by a missed deadline and then by a train accident, the monument was installed just a week before the exhibition closed.
paragraphs.zh: 尽管如此，在展览和整个国家中占据一席之地仍然是美国黑人继续追求的目标。非裔卫理公会教会的成员委托建造一座纪念碑，纪念理查德·艾伦：他是早期美国共和国时期费城的知名黑人，也是该教派的创始人。该团体宣布道："我们打算在1876年离开费城，如同1776年的英雄一样，带着实现崇高目标的坚定决心。并且在1976年，我们希望我们的后裔围绕这座纪念碑聚集，对我们留下的榜样心怀感激，泪流满面，并称我们有福。"该纪念碑最初因错过截止日期而推迟，随后又因一场火车事故延误，最终是在展览闭幕前仅一周安装完毕。

paragraphs.en: In Allen’s absence, the most prominent “memorialization” for Black Americans was a restaurant, not a monument or a pavilion. When a white Atlanta businessman proposed to open a “Restaurant of the South,” centennial commissioners welcomed the prospect. The waitstaff would be among the only Black employees on the fairgrounds (Black workers were mostly excluded from the centennial), and their job would include playing enslaved people on a plantation, singing “quaint melodies,” and strumming the banjo. Northern fetishization of southern slavery was nothing new; the minstrel show had become popular in northern cities in the decades before the Civil War. But the success of the restaurant and its apparent nostalgia for slavery seemed to complete a notable shift in national sentiment since Appomattox.
paragraphs.zh: 在艾伦缺席的情况下，美国黑人最突出的“纪念”并不是纪念碑或展馆，而是一家餐厅。一位白人亚特兰大商人提出开设“南方餐厅”后，百年展览委员会欢迎了这一设想。餐厅的服务员将是会场里少数几位黑人雇员之一（黑人劳动者在世博中大多被排斥在外），他们的职责包括在场景中扮演种植园里的奴隶，演唱“古风旋律”，并弹奏班卓琴。北方对南方奴隶制的迷恋并不是什么新鲜事；在南北战争前的几十年里，小丑式黑脸表演在北方城市已广受欢迎。但这家餐厅的大获成功以及它对奴隶制所展现的怀旧情绪，似乎标志着自阿波马托克斯以来全国舆论的一次显著转变。

paragraphs.en: From the November 2025 issue: Clint Smith on telling the full story of Colonial Williamsburg
paragraphs.zh: 摘自《2025年11月刊》：Clint Smith 解读《威廉斯堡殖民地》完整故事

paragraphs.en: Many northerners (Republicans, at least) had linked Black freedom to national progress immediately after the Civil War. That connection had all but faded by 1876, amid violent campaigns to “redeem” southern states from Republican rule and Black voters. As Bordewich relates in a harrowing account, those efforts had turned to South Carolina in the summer of 1876. Well aware that they were in the minority in their state, South Carolina’s redeemers had been waiting for a chance to “provoke a riot and teach the negroes a lesson,” as the future governor and senator Ben Tillman recalled. “It was generally believed,” Tillman wrote, “that nothing but bloodshed and a good deal of it could so well answer the purpose of redeeming the state from negro and carpetbag rule.”
paragraphs.zh: 内战结束后，许多北方人（至少是共和党人）曾立即将黑人自由与国家进步联系在一起。到1876年，这种联系几乎已经消失，在这期间，南方各州内部发动了暴力“重夺”运动，以摆脱共和党的统治以及黑人选民。正如博迪维奇在一份惊心动魄的记述中所说，这些行动在1876年夏天蔓延到了南卡罗来纳州。南卡罗来纳州的“拯救者”们清楚自己在州内是少数派，便在等待一个时机，以“煽动暴乱并给黑人一个教训”，正如后来成为州长兼参议员的本·蒂尔曼回忆的那样。“蒂尔曼写道：'普遍的看法是，只有流血，而且要流不少血，才能最好地达成让这个州摆脱黑人与‘行李包’政权的目的。'」

paragraphs.en: The redeemers had their opening when two white men in a carriage confronted a Black militia marching in a Fourth of July parade in the town of Hamburg. When the two men returned for a court date four days later, they came with a mob and a cannon. The militiamen did not appear at the courthouse, but took shelter instead in a nearby building that housed their weapons and ammunition. In the ensuing standoff, the mob (including Tillman and his rifle club) began shelling the building. When the militiamen were driven from the building, the mob killed six of the men, as well as the town’s Black marshal, cutting out his tongue. The massacre was just a piece of what became a successful campaign to oust the Republican governor and legislature. The following month, a Black South Carolina Republican wrote to President Ulysses S. Grant begging for federal protection after another armed white group attacked a party meeting and demanded that the men “give up the flag” or be shot.
paragraphs.zh: 汉堡镇在7月4日游行中，两名白人男子在马车里拦住一支黑人民兵，由此引爆了“救赎者”行动。四天后，这两人按约定的开庭日期返回时，带着一群暴徒和一门大炮回来了。民兵并未出现在法院，而是躲进一栋附近的楼里，那是他们存放武器和弹药的地方。随后的对峙中，暴徒（包括蒂尔曼和他的枪械社团）开始向该建筑开炮。民兵被赶出建筑后，暴徒又杀害了其中六名男子，以及镇上的黑人警长，并把他的舌头割下。此次屠杀只是后来那场成功驱逐州共和党州长和立法机构的运动的一部分。次月，一位南卡罗来纳州的黑人共和党人给尤利西斯·S·格兰特总统写信，恳求联邦保护，因为又有一支武装白人小队袭击了一个党务会议，并逼迫与会者“交出党旗”，否则就枪毙。

paragraphs.en: At the centennial’s closing, on November 10, 1876, there was no providential break in the rain. Those on hand for the ceremonies packed into Judges’ Hall for valedictory reflections and final rounds of Handel’s “Hallelujah” chorus, Wagner’s march, and a rendition of “America.” Grant declared the exhibition over, and a telegrapher tapped out instructions to the Corliss Engine’s operator to halt the great machine.
paragraphs.zh: 在百年庆典结束时，即1876年11月10日，雨并未出现“上帝般”的停顿。出席仪式的人们聚集在法官大厅里，进行结束式感想，并再次合唱了韩德尔的《哈利路亚》合唱、瓦格纳进行曲，以及《美国人》的一段演奏。格兰特宣布展览结束，一位电报员向柯利斯发动机的操作员发出停机指令，令这台巨大的机器停转。

paragraphs.en: Three days earlier, on Election Day, the great machine of American self-government had also come to a halt. That year’s presidential contest pitted New York’s Democratic governor, Samuel J. Tilden, against Ohio’s Republican governor, Rutherford B. Hayes. Neither was a man of great charisma—Tilden shy; Hayes upright, teetotal, and vague—but everyone knew what was at least nominally at stake in the election. In addition to taking on the widespread corruption with which the Republicans had become associated, Tilden promised an end to Reconstruction. Hayes paid lip service to the rights of former slaves, while also voicing his commitment to reconciliation between the North and the South.
paragraphs.zh: 三天前，也就是选举日，美国自我治理这台“伟大机器”也曾停摆。那一年的总统竞选，让纽约民主党州长塞缪尔·J·蒂尔登对阵俄亥俄共和党州长拉瑟福德·B·海斯。两位候选人都不是特别有魅力的人——蒂尔登内向，海斯正直、禁酒并且言辞含糊——但人们都清楚这场选举至少表面上关乎什么。当年竞选中，蒂尔登不仅主张整治共和党人广泛卷入的腐败，还承诺结束“重建”时期。海斯口头上支持前奴隶的权利，同时也表达了他致力于实现南北和解的承诺。

paragraphs.en: From the April 1877 issue: James A. Garfield on a century of Congress
paragraphs.zh: 《美国国会百年》：詹姆斯·A·加菲尔德（摘自1877年4月刊）

paragraphs.en: Though the choice was not exactly stark, the outcome was opaque. Bordewich offers a concise account of the tangled mess that followed. Tilden won the popular vote and stood on the precipice of claiming the Electoral College, but given rampant problems and irregularities, the nation “teetered on the edge of a constitutional abyss,” as Bordewich writes. The result came down to three southern states—Florida, South Carolina, and Louisiana—in which both parties claimed victories in state and national races.
paragraphs.zh: 尽管选择并非完全对立，结果却模糊不清。博德威奇简明扼要地梳理了随后出现的复杂混乱。蒂尔登赢得了普选票，并站在即将拿下选举人团的边缘，但由于泛滥的违规和不规范行为，国家“摇摇欲坠，濒临宪法深渊”，正如博德威奇所写。结果最后落在三个南方州——佛罗里达、南卡罗来纳和路易斯安那——上，两个政党都声称在州内和全国性选举中获胜。

paragraphs.en: An 1878 painting of a congressional hearing addressing the disputed 1876 election between Samuel J. Tilden and Rutherford B. Hayes (Cornelia Adele Strong Fassett / Three Lions / Hulton Archive / Getty)
paragraphs.zh: 1878 年一幅描绘国会听证会的画作，内容是塞缪尔·J·蒂尔登与拉瑟福德·B·海斯之间有争议的1876年大选（Cornelia Adele Strong Fassett / Three Lions / Hulton Archive / Getty）

paragraphs.en: Absent the violence and intimidation visited upon Black voters, the Republicans would likely have won each state, but winners were impossible to determine. Florida ended up with three separate vote counts. In South Carolina, where the number of votes exceeded the number of eligible voters, two legislatures and two governors vied for control and haggled over the presidency. Louisiana’s electoral commissioner put the state’s returns up for sale to the highest bidder, while a monitoring commission tossed out the results from 15 parishes in which the fraud and violence had been particularly egregious.
paragraphs.zh: 如果没有对黑人选民的暴力和恐吓，共和党人很可能会赢得每一个州，但赢家却难以确认。佛罗里达最终出现了三套不同的票数统计。南卡罗来纳州出现了选民人数超过合格选民人数的现象，两个州议会和两位州长争夺控制权，并就总统职位讨价还价。路易斯安那州的选举专员将该州的选票结果拿出来拍卖给出价最高者，而一个监督委员会则否决了15个教区的结果，原因是这些地区的舞弊和暴力行为尤其恶劣。

paragraphs.en: Another civil war loomed. While the White League in Louisiana threatened to attack the statehouse, and representatives of rival governments, all heavily armed, faced off in South Carolina, former Union General George B. McClellan, a Tilden supporter, talked of marching on Washington at the head of an army. Resolution came only through shadowy negotiations and political compromises. Congress created a special Electoral Commission—composed of senators, representatives, and Supreme Court justices—to determine the outcome, but its deliberations quickly broke along partisan lines. In a series of backroom dealings, Democrats agreed to accept Hayes’s election if federal troops were withdrawn from the South. Reconstruction, already heading toward a violent end in places like Hamburg, came to an official close as Hayes removed the last federal troops from the South, seven weeks after his inauguration.
paragraphs.zh: 另一场内战几乎重演。正当路易斯安那白人联盟威胁要攻击州议事厅、南卡罗来纳州对峙的两套对立政府代表都身着重武装时，前联邦将军乔治·B·麦克莱伦，一位蒂尔登的支持者，曾谈及率军进军华盛顿。和解最终仅通过秘密谈判和政治交易才得以实现。国会设立了一个由参议员、众议员和最高法院大法官组成的特别选举委员会来裁定结果，但其审议很快就按党派分裂。经过一连串幕后交易，民主党同意承认海斯当选，总提是联邦军队从南方撤出。重建时期，本已在汉堡等地走向暴力结局，最终在海斯上任七周后撤走南方最后一批联邦军队时正式走向终点。

paragraphs.en: Not long before the Centennial Exhibition ended, a Massachusetts man published a poem in a Washington, D.C., newspaper telling “The Story of Hamburg,” just a few columns over from a grim run of headlines leading an article on continuing violence associated with the election. The poem isn’t in Bordewich’s book, but, like the stark contrasts in its pages, the lines speak to our moment. “Let others tell of the nation’s glory,” the poet began; his attention would be elsewhere:
paragraphs.zh: 在美国建国一百周年纪念展览结束之前不久，一位来自马萨诸塞州的男子在华盛顿特区的一家报纸上发表了一首诗《汉堡的故事》，位置紧挨着一篇关于与选举有关的持续暴力事件的头条新闻。该诗不在博尔德威奇的书中，但就像其页面中的鲜明对照，这些诗句也在回应我们这个时代。“别人去讲这国家的光荣吧，”诗人起笔道；他的目光会在别处：

paragraphs.en: On the 250th anniversary, we are no less caught between the glory and the shame than our forebears were on the 100th. Since the 1960s, the writing of American history has largely been a project of recovering and reckoning with conquest and its legacies, racism and the limits of democratic practice, and horrific events like the Hamburg Massacre. The aim of the official anniversary proceedings embodied in President Trump’s 2025 executive order has been to “restore truth and sanity to American history”—and, in the process, to recover the glory and forget the shame. That effort was vividly realized in Philadelphia earlier this year, when the National Park Service removed a slavery exhibit from the President’s House in Independence National Historical Park. A federal judge’s order to restore the exhibit is now being appealed, and the fight over where to look—and how to look—at our past remains the crux of our commemorations.
paragraphs.zh: 在250周年之际，我们与先辈在第100个年头时一样，仍处在荣耀与耻辱之间。自20世纪60年代以来，美国历史书写大体上就是一项对征服及其遗产、种族主义和民主实践局限，以及汉堡大屠杀等惨痛事件的追索与清算。特朗普总统2025年行政令所体现的官方周年纪念纲领，旨在“把美国历史中的真相与理性拉回原位”——并在此过程中，重建荣耀、抹去耻辱。正是这一点在今年早些时候于费城被鲜活展现，当时国家公园局从独立国家历史公园内的总统官邸撤下了一处关于奴隶制的展览。联邦法官要求恢复该展览的命令正在上诉中，而关于我们如何观看、以及应在哪里观看我们过去的争论，仍然是我们纪念活动的核心。

paragraphs.en: Though we’ll mostly be looking back at 1776 this year, Bordewich has done a great service in calling our attention to 1876. The soundness of our democratic machinery is again in doubt; we, too, wonder at the power of new technology, and what it means for work; race remains a source of conflict and a tool of power. Bordewich is as cautious in drawing such parallels as he is in spelling out the lessons that might lie in the juxtapositions of fairground and background. It’s up to us to hear the echoes, and to make sense of the glory and the shame.
paragraphs.zh: 虽然今年我们大多会回望1776，但博尔德威奇有功效地引导我们注意到1876。我们民主机器的稳健性再次受到质疑；我们也同样在思考新技术的力量，以及它对劳工意味着什么；种族仍然是冲突的来源，也是权力的工具。博尔德威奇在类比这些问题时同样谨慎，他也同样谨慎地指出了在游乐场景与其背后背景之间的并置中可能蕴含的教训。现在由我们来听见这些回响，并理解其中的荣耀与耻辱。

paragraphs.en: This article appears in the June 2026 print edition with the headline “How America Celebrated Its 100th Birthday.”
paragraphs.zh: 这篇文章发表于2026年6月的印刷版，标题是“美国如何庆祝其100周年生日”。
